result:
# Proposal Coverage Check

## Current project state (provided by user)
PROJECT STATE: Greenfield. Nothing built yet. About to initialize planning artifacts (PROJECT.md, REQUIREMENTS.md, ROADMAP.md) for an AI-driven SEO ranking predictor & recommendation system.

INTENDED SCOPE:
- Domain: Developer documentation pages (docs.python.org, react.dev, Stripe docs, MDN, etc.)
- Scale: ~1500 scraped pages — narrowed-domain pivot from the original 50K-row proposal, executing CIS 2450 TA Ricky's 3/29/2026 email guidance to focus on a specific topic area when full-scale collection is rate-limited. Pivot will be cited in README.md and data/README.md.
- Prediction target: Binary classification — does this documentation page appear in the top-10 Google SERP results for its primary topic query (query derived from the page's <title> tag)? Single, specific, measurable target — explicitly addresses Shivani's feedback that the original proposal was too vague.

PLANNED DATA SOURCES (2 distinct):
1. Scraped doc-site HTML (async scraper, rate-limited, robots.txt-respecting)
2. SerpApi free tier OR Brave Search API for SERP rankings ground truth

PLANNED FEATURES (well above 7-10 minimum):
- Content: TF-IDF, keyword density, readability (Flesch), text length
- Metadata: title length, meta description length/presence
- Structural: header counts (H1/H2/H3), internal/external link counts, image count, alt-text coverage
- Graph: PageRank, HITS hub/authority, in-degree, out-degree, clustering coefficient (NetworkX directed graph from internal links)

PLANNED MODEL SWEEP (8 models, all with documented justification):
1. Logistic regression (baseline)
2. Random Forest
3. Gradient Boosting (sklearn)
4. XGBoost
5. LightGBM
6. CatBoost
7. SVM with RBF
8. Shallow MLP in PyTorch (hits the Colab/checkpoint requirement)

All using StratifiedKFold CV + RandomizedSearchCV. Unified evaluator returns precision/recall/F1/ROC-AUC/PR-AUC/confusion matrix. Final comparison table + clear winner + SHAP analysis on winner. MODELING_DECISIONS.md will log every kept/dropped decision with prior-result reasoning.

PLANNED EDA (5 rubric-targeted charts in notebooks/01_eda.ipynb, exported as ≥300 DPI PNG to assets/charts/):
- Class balance (justifies F1/ROC-AUC over accuracy)
- Distribution plots per numeric feature with top-10 vs not-top-10 hue
- Feature-vs-target correlation heatmap
- PageRank distribution histogram (log-scale x)
- TF-IDF top terms by class
Each chart gets a markdown caption explicitly tying it to a modeling decision.

PLANNED DASHBOARD (Streamlit, 20 pts):
- URL input → live feature extraction (scrape on demand)
- Top-10 probability gauge/progress bar
- SHAP top-5 contributing features panel
- Recommendation panel (actionable text suggestions)
- What-if simulator with sliders + live re-prediction (rubric differentiator)
- Custom CSS for typography
- Sidebar nav: Predict, Recommendations, What-if, About
- Demo data fallback so live demo never breaks

PLANNED PRESENTATION (10 pts, 8-10 min hard window, both members speak):
- PDF slides, 8-10 min recording, no code on slides, no sped-up audio
- Sections: objective+value (30s), data+pivot (1m), 3-5 EDA charts (2m), modeling progression (3m), implications (1m), limitations (1m), live demo (1m)
- ≥300 DPI charts, seaborn whitegrid or custom matplotlib stylesheet

PLANNED CODEBASE STRUCTURE (modular, docstrings + type hints throughout):
/
├── README.md (CIS 2450 main, maps each rubric req to file path)
├── readme.txt (NETS 1500 one-pager)
├── USER_MANUAL.md (NETS 1500 user manual w/ screenshots)
├── MODELING_DECISIONS.md (living log)
├── requirements.txt, .env.example
├── data/{raw,interim,processed}/, data/README.md
├── src/{scraping,features,graph,models,recommendations,dashboard}/
├── notebooks/{01_eda,02_graph_analysis,03_model_comparison}.ipynb
├── tests/test_{features,graph,models}.py
└── presentation/{slides.pdf, recording.mp4}

PLANNED PHASE ORDERING (for ROADMAP.md):
Phase 1: Data layer (scraping + SERP) + 2-source documentation
Phase 2: Feature engineering (content/metadata/structural)
Phase 3: Graph layer (PageRank/HITS) + graph features
Phase 4: EDA + 3-5 rubric charts
Phase 5: Modeling sweep (all 8 models with justification)
Phase 6: Recommendation system + SHAP
Phase 7: Streamlit dashboard with what-if simulator
Phase 8: NETS 1500 deliverables (USER_MANUAL.md + readme.txt) — ship to Gradescope tonight 4/29
Phase 9: CIS 2450 polish (README, MODELING_DECISIONS.md cleanup, codebase review)
Phase 10: Slide deck + recording (Ayush leads, Rahil reviews) — by 4/30 11:59pm

DEADLINE CONTEXT:
- Today is 2026-04-29
- NETS 1500 (85 pts) due TONIGHT 4/29 — minimum viable
- CIS 2450 (143 pts) due TOMORROW 4/30 11:59pm — 90% of effort goes here

WORK BREAKDOWN:
- Rahil: scraping, feature engineering, graph layer, baseline+tree+boosting models, dashboard backend
- Ayush: EDA + visualizations, kernel+neural models, evaluation framework, recommendations, dashboard frontend+polish, slide deck

QUESTION FOR THE COMPLIANCE TOOL:
Please return the full CIS 2450 rubric, NETS 1500 rubric, original proposal text, and both TAs' (Ricky and Shivani) email feedback so I can build planning artifacts directly against them. Flag any gaps in the planned scope above versus what the rubrics actually require. Treat anything missing as a P0 blocker we need to add to the roadmap before planning Phase 1.

---

## Original proposal text
AI-Driven  SEO  Optimization  and  Visibility  Prediction  
System
 
I.  Group  Members  and  Responsibilities  
Rahil  Patel  will  primarily  focus  on  collecting  and  preparing  the  data.  This  includes  building  the  web  
scraping
 
pipeline,
 
integrating
 
any
 
APIs
 
we
 
use,
 
and
 
designing
 
meaningful
 
features
 
from
 
webpage
 
content,
 
metadata,
 
and
 
structure.
 
He
 
will
 
also
 
work
 
on
 
implementing
 
the
 
baseline
 
and
 
advanced
 
models
 
and
 
helping
 
connect
 
the
 
modeling
 
pipeline
 
to
 
the
 
dashboard
 
backend.
 
 
Ayush  Tripathi  will  focus  more  on  analysis  and  presentation.  He  will  handle  exploratory  data  analysis  and  
visualizations,
 
evaluate
 
model
 
performance,
 
and
 
carry
 
out
 
hyperparameter
 
tuning.
 
In
 
addition,
 
he
 
will
 
work
 
on
 
the
 
recommendation
 
system
 
that
 
suggests
 
improvements
 
to
 
webpages
 
and
 
will
 
lead
 
the
 
development
 
of
 
the
 
dashboard
 
interface
 
and
 
user
 
interaction.
 
II.  Data  Sources  
This  project  will  use  two  main  sources  of  data.  First,  we  will  collect  raw  webpage  data  by  scraping  
publicly
 
available
 
websites
 
such
 
as
 
blogs,
 
articles,
 
and
 
documentation
 
pages.
 
From
 
these
 
pages,
 
we
 
will
 
extract
 
the
 
HTML
 
structure,
 
including
 
headers
 
and
 
links,
 
along
 
with
 
metadata
 
such
 
as
 
titles
 
and
 
descriptions,
 
and
 
the
 
main
 
textual
 
content.
 
In
 
addition
 
to
 
this,
 
we
 
will
 
use
 
a
 
search
 
engine
 
API,
 
such
 
as
 
Bing
 
or
 
another
 
free-tier
 
SERP
 
API,
 
to
 
gather
 
information
 
about
 
how
 
these
 
pages
 
rank
 
for
 
specific
 
queries.
 
This
 
will
 
allow
 
us
 
to
 
construct
 
a
 
target
 
variable
 
that
 
reflects
 
SEO
 
visibility,
 
such
 
as
 
whether
 
a
 
page
 
appears
 
in
 
the
 
top
 
search
 
results.
 
We
 
will
 
ensure
 
that
 
all
 
data
 
collection
 
is
 
done
 
responsibly,
 
respecting
 
rate
 
limits
 
and
 
the
 
terms
 
of
 
service
 
of
 
each
 
website.
 
III.  Objective  and  Value  
The  main  goal  of  this  project  is  to  understand  what  factors  contribute  to  strong  SEO  performance  and  to  
build
 
a
 
system
 
that
 
can
 
predict
 
how
 
well
 
a
 
webpage
 
will
 
rank
 
based
 
on
 
those
 
factors.
 
Beyond
 
just
 
prediction,
 
we
 
want
 
to
 
make
 
the
 
system
 
useful
 
by
 
generating
 
concrete
 
suggestions
 
that
 
can
 
help
 
improve
 
a
 
page’s
 
visibility.
 
This
 
project
 
is
 
interesting
 
because
 
it
 
reflects
 
a
 
real-world
 
problem
 
that
 
many
 
companies
 
and
 
content
 
creators
 
care
 
about.
 
It
 
also
 
involves
 
working
 
with
 
both
 
structured
 
data,
 
such
 
as
 
HTML
 
elements,
 
and
 
unstructured
 
text
 
data.
 
Finally,
 
it
 
allows
 
us
 
to
 
build
 
a
 
system
 
that
 
not
 
only
 
analyzes
 
performance
 
but
 
also
 
provides
 
actionable
 
insights
 
for
 
improvement.
 
IV.  Modeling  Plan  
We  will  approach  this  as  a  classification  problem,  where  the  goal  is  to  predict  whether  a  webpage  appears  
in
 
the
 
top
 
10
 
search
 
results
 
for
 
a
 
given
 
query.
 
This
 
provides
 
a
 
clear
 
and
 
meaningful
 
target
 
variable
 
tied
 
to
 

SEO  performance.  We  will  begin  with  a  logistic  regression  model  as  a  baseline,  and  then  move  to  more  
complex
 
models
 
such
 
as
 
random
 
forests
 
and
 
gradient
 
boosting
 
methods
 
like
 
XGBoost
 
or
 
LightGBM.
 
These
 
models
 
will
 
allow
 
us
 
to
 
capture
 
more
 
complex
 
relationships
 
in
 
the
 
data.
 
To
 
evaluate
 
performance,
 
we
 
will
 
use
 
metrics
 
such
 
as
 
precision,
 
recall,
 
and
 
F1-score,
 
especially
 
since
 
the
 
data
 
may
 
be
 
imbalanced.
 
We
 
will
 
also
 
perform
 
hyperparameter
 
tuning
 
using
 
methods
 
like
 
randomized
 
search
 
to
 
improve
 
model
 
performance.
 
A.  Feature  Engineering  
A  key  part  of  this  project  will  be  designing  meaningful  features.  We  will  extract  
content-based
 
features
 
such
 
as
 
keyword
 
density,
 
text
 
length,
 
readability,
 
and
 
possibly
 
vector
 
representations
 
like
 
TF-IDF
 
or
 
embeddings.
 
We
 
will
 
also
 
include
 
metadata
 
features
 
such
 
as
 
title
 
length
 
and
 
whether
 
important
 
keywords
 
appear
 
in
 
the
 
title
 
or
 
description.
 
In
 
addition,
 
we
 
will
 
create
 
structural
 
features
 
from
 
the
 
HTML,
 
including
 
the
 
number
 
of
 
headers,
 
links,
 
and
 
images,
 
as
 
well
 
as
 
whether
 
images
 
include
 
alt
 
text.
 
We
 
may
 
also
 
include
 
simple
 
technical
 
features
 
such
 
as
 
URL
 
length
 
and
 
structure.
 
Organizing
 
features
 
in
 
this
 
way
 
will
 
help
 
us
 
better
 
understand
 
what
 
aspects
 
of
 
a
 
page
 
contribute
 
most
 
to
 
SEO
 
performance.
 
B.  Recommendation  System  
As  part  of  the  final  system,  we  will  build  a  recommendation  component  integrated  into  
the
 
dashboard.
 
This
 
system
 
will
 
take
 
in
 
a
 
webpage
 
along
 
with
 
its
 
extracted
 
features
 
and
 
model
 
predictions,
 
and
 
generate
 
suggestions
 
for
 
improvement.
 
These
 
suggestions
 
will
 
focus
 
on
 
practical
 
changes,
 
such
 
as
 
improving
 
the
 
title
 
to
 
include
 
relevant
 
keywords,
 
adjusting
 
content
 
length,
 
adding
 
headers
 
for
 
better
 
structure,
 
or
 
improving
 
metadata
 
completeness.
 
The
 
goal
 
is
 
to
 
make
 
the
 
output
 
directly
 
useful
 
rather
 
than
 
just
 
descriptive.
 
If
 
time
 
permits,
 
we
 
will
 
also
 
implement
 
a
 
simple
 
simulation
 
step
 
where
 
we
 
apply
 
suggested
 
changes
 
to
 
the
 
feature
 
set
 
and
 
show
 
how
 
the
 
model’s
 
prediction
 
would
 
change.
 
This
 
would
 
allow
 
users
 
to
 
see
 
the
 
potential
 
impact
 
of
 
different
 
improvements.
 V.  Challenges  
There  are  several  challenges  we  anticipate.  Collecting  reliable  data  can  be  difficult  due  to  rate  
limits
 
and
 
inconsistencies
 
across
 
websites.
 
Constructing
 
a
 
meaningful
 
target
 
variable
 
based
 
on
 
search
 
rankings
 
may
 
also
 
require
 
careful
 
handling.
 
Feature
 
extraction
 
from
 
HTML
 
can
 
be
 
noisy,
 
and
 
there
 
are
 
important
 
SEO
 
factors,
 
such
 
as
 
backlinks,
 
that
 
we
 
may
 
not
 
be
 
able
 
to
 
capture.
 
This
 
means
 
our
 
model
 
will
 
not
 
fully
 
represent
 
all
 
aspects
 
of
 
ranking.
 
Another
 
challenge
 
is
 
choosing
 
appropriate
 
evaluation
 
methods,
 
particularly
 
if
 
the
 
dataset
 
is
 
imbalanced.
 
Finally,
 
we
 
need
 
to
 
ensure
 
that
 
the
 
recommendation
 
system
 
produces
 
suggestions
 
that
 
are
 
consistent
 
and
 
actually
 
useful,
 
rather
 
than
 
generic.

## CIS 2450 TA feedback (Ricky)
Rahil Patel <rahilp07@seas.upenn.edu>
CIS 2450 - Project Proposal Feedback3 messages
Ricky Gong <gong8@seas.upenn.edu> Sun, Mar 29, 2026 at 6:34 PM
To: "tripath1@seas.upenn.edu" <tripath1@seas.upenn.edu>, "rahilp07@seas.upenn.edu" <rahilp07@seas.upenn.edu>
Hi Rahil and Ayush,
My name is Ricky and I will be your project mentor for the final project. I will help you plan and
implement a successful project by providing feedback and answering any questions you might
have.
Please use me as your first point-of-contact on staff for any questions unique to your project.
Feel free to reach out by emailing me here. To ask general questions about the project (e.g.
confirming a deadline), make public posts on Ed per standard course policy.
I enjoyed reading your project proposal. I am excited to work with you to build your project in the
coming weeks. I had a few comments about the proposed idea:
Model Appropriateness/Complexity: The classification pipeline (logistic regression to random
forest to XGBoost/LightGBM) is a good fit for this task. The feature engineering plan is well
thought out, and breaking features into content, metadata, and structural categories gives you a
clear roadmap. The recommendation system component is also a nice touch since it makes the
output actually useful rather than just predictive.
Scope: The main risk here is data collection scale. Acquiring 50K+ labeled webpages with
corresponding SERP ranking data through a free-tier API could be slow and rate-limited. I'd
prioritize getting the data pipeline working first, validate your data volume early, and have a
backup plan (e.g., focusing on a specific domain or topic area) in case the full dataset is hard to
collect.
Let me know if you have any questions about this feedback or the project in general.
Proposal Score: 5/5
Next Step: Next up would be the intermediate check-in, for which we will meet once sometime
between April 6th to April 12th. You can schedule a meeting here. Choose a time that works for
all your group members as all group members must be present. Make sure to schedule a
meeting by April 6th.
The final project deadline is on April 30th. Please also reach out to me if there are any issues as
you move forward with the project, and I'll be happy to help. As a reminder, please remember to
read both the project details AND project rubric documents carefully. All the best on the project!
Best, 
Ricky

## NETS 1500 TA feedback (Shivani)
Rahil Patel <rahilp07@seas.upenn.edu>
[ACTION REQUIRED] NETS 1500 - HW5 Project Proposal Feedback1 message
Shivani Shreedhar <shivani9@seas.upenn.edu> Sat, Apr 11, 2026 at 7:48 PM
To: Rahil Patel <rahilp07@seas.upenn.edu>, Ayush Tripathi <tripath1@seas.upenn.edu>
Cc: Swapneel Sheth <swapneel@seas.upenn.edu>, Brandon Tsai <brantsai@seas.upenn.edu>, Logan Brassington
<logbrass@seas.upenn.edu>, Patricia Columbia-Walsh <pcwalsh@sas.upenn.edu>
Hi Ayush and Rahil,
I'm Shivani and I will be your project mentor for the HW5 final project. I will help you plan and create a 
successful project by providing feedback and answering any questions you might have.
Please use me as your first point-of-contact on staff for any questions unique to your project. Feel free to 
reach out by making a private post on Ed or emailing me here. To ask general questions about the 
project (e.g. confirming a deadline), make posts on Ed per standard course policy.
I enjoyed reading your project proposal, and am excited to work with you to build your project in the 
coming weeks. I had a few comments about the proposed idea:
Great job coming up with a clear real-world application and integrating the class concepts! 
I think your deliverables are concrete and well defined and I am excited to see your models and 
dashboard in addition to your report. 
The main thing to improve is specificity about the prediction target. For example instead of saying 
“predict how well a webpage will rank” say exactly what you predict and what metrics you are 
using etc
I would also make sure you clearly explain where your webpage data will come from and specify 
which datasets or webpages you are planning on using. Also consider using public datasets, APIs, 
or your own crawled sample from from web domains if large-scale scraping becomes difficult
I would also specify more about which specific “graph algorithms” you plan on integrating into your 
project. But overall you guys did a great job! 
Let me know if you have any questions about this feedback or the project in general. The final project 
deadline is on 4/29. Please also reach out to me if there are any issues as you move forward with the 
project, and I’ll be happy to help. My office hours are typically 9-11 on Tuesday but you can see Ed 
Discussion for the latest OH schedule. All the best on the project!
Best,
Shivani Shreedhar 


---

## Instructions for grading

Walk through the proposal section by section and produce a checklist with
status for each promised item: ✅ covered, 🟡 partial, ❌ missing.

Sections to check at minimum:
1. **Group members & responsibilities** — Did Rahil cover his (data collection,
   scraping, APIs, feature engineering, baseline + advanced models, dashboard
   backend)? Did Ayush cover his (EDA, viz, model evaluation, hyperparameter
   tuning, recommendation system, dashboard frontend)?
2. **Data sources** — webpage scraping pipeline + SERP API. Per Ricky's feedback,
   data scale is the #1 risk. Confirm 50K+ rows after cleaning (CIS 2450 hard
   requirement) OR a documented narrower-domain backup plan.
3. **Objective & value** — classification of top-10 SERP rank + actionable
   recommendations.
4. **Modeling plan** — logistic regression baseline → random forest → XGBoost /
   LightGBM. Precision / recall / F1 (since data is imbalanced). Hyperparameter
   tuning via randomized search.
5. **Feature engineering** — content (keyword density, length, readability,
   TF-IDF/embeddings), metadata (title length, keywords in title/description),
   structural (header count, link count, image count, alt text), technical
   (URL length/structure).
6. **Recommendation system** — generates suggestions tied to model predictions;
   bonus if simulation step shows predicted impact of changes.
7. **Challenges acknowledgment** — rate limits, target variable construction,
   noisy HTML feature extraction, missing backlink data, imbalance handling.

Then specifically address the TA feedback:
- **Shivani (NETS 1500)**: Did you make the prediction target specific (exact
  metric, not just "ranks well")? Did you specify data sources / datasets /
  domains? Did you identify which graph algorithms are integrated?
- **Ricky (CIS 2450)**: Did you validate data volume early? If 50K+ was
  infeasible, is there documented evidence you executed the narrower-domain
  backup plan?

For each item: state status, cite specifically what in the project_summary
supports the conclusion (or note "no evidence in summary"), and give a concrete
next action if not fully covered.


================================================================================

## Full CIS 2450 instructions & rubric
Final Project
CIS 2450: Big Data Analytics

I. Introduction to the Final Project

The objective of this project is for you to show that you are able to apply the topics in this course
in a real-world setting. You are welcome to use concepts beyond the scope of the course in
addition to concepts covered in the course; however, a major portion of your project needs to be
based on this course’s material. You are not allowed to reuse a project from another course. This
project is a good opportunity to create something that you could add to your resume, post online
in your personal GitHub, or share on LinkedIn.

Projects will be done in groups of 2 people — no exceptions. Make sure all team members are
in time zones where you all can work together without problems. If you are unable to find a

IX. Data Source Requirements

Amount of Data:

It is important to make sure your data source provides enough data to support your downstream
modelling and analysis tasks. A high-quality data source should provide around 50,000+ rows.
Please note that this is the threshold for the number of rows which should be a part of the
analysis, so rows removed during data cleaning should not be counted. You should have 50,000+
rows after the data cleaning.

Complexity of Data:

Additionally, your data source should provide enough interesting fields and features to look at
apart from the target classification/regression feature. We can not give a number for this as
sometimes a few valuable API response fields can hold a rich amount of information and
sometimes we need more features to get a complete picture. Generally, you should have at least
7-10 columns. But this number is more flexible and can vary widely.

X. Tips for Success

e Please get started early, and feel free to come to office hours or post on Ed with any
questions.

Once you’ve locked down a good data source, scrape data from it and store it locally.
Use proper version control via Git to ensure a smooth collaborative development
process.

e Ifyour scraped dataset is large, you are encouraged to download your “cleaned data” and
import it midpoint to avoid having to re-process the data every time you restart the
session.

© Note: You still need to submit all the codes you used for data gathering (if
applicable), data cleaning, data integration, data wrangling, and EDA.

e Email Etiquette Reminder: When communicating with your TA about the project, make
sure you cc all your teammates to ensure everyone is on the same page.

## Grading framework

The course allocates 143 total points across the project. Evaluate the
current state against each component and assign a tentative score with
justification.

### 1. Proposal (5 pts) — already submitted, scored 5/5 per Ricky's feedback.

### 2. Intermediate Check-in (5 pts) — confirm meeting was scheduled and
attended between April 6th–12th.

### 3. Codebase (83 pts) — the heaviest weight. Evaluate:
   - **Modularity** — is the code organized into clear modules (data/,
     features/, models/, dashboard/)? Or is it one giant notebook?
   - **Documentation** — docstrings, README, inline comments where needed.
   - **Readability** — naming, formatting, no dead code.
   - **Equal contributions** — git history shows commits from both members.
   - **Version control** — proper branching/commits, not a single dump commit.
   - **EDA** — present, with charts that informed modeling decisions.
   - **Modeling** — baseline → advanced progression, evaluation metrics
     appropriate for imbalanced data (precision/recall/F1 + ROC-AUC),
     hyperparameter tuning documented.
   - **Interactive demo support** — codebase can actually run the dashboard.

### 4. Dashboard Demo (20 pts):
   - Interactive (not just static charts).
   - Showcases full capabilities (input a webpage, get prediction +
     recommendations).
   - Comparable in polish to the homework dashboards.

### 5. Presentation & Recording (10 pts):
   - 8–10 minutes (HARD limit — outside this range = penalty).
   - Both team members present.
   - Slides submitted as PDF.
   - No code shown.
   - No sped-up audio (-2 pts if detected).
   - Covers: objective, data, EDA highlights (3–5 charts), modeling results,
     implications, challenges/future work.

### Hard requirements (failing any = automatic 0):
   - Data is free, publicly available, and legal.
   - At least 2 distinct data sources.
   - 50K+ rows after cleaning (or documented narrower-domain pivot).
   - 7–10+ feature columns.
   - AI usage documented in detail in deliverables.

For each component: assign a tentative score, explain the reasoning, and
list specific gaps to close before April 30th.

# NETS 1500 HW5 Rubric Evaluation

## Current project state
PROJECT STATE: Greenfield. Nothing built yet. About to initialize planning artifacts (PROJECT.md, REQUIREMENTS.md, ROADMAP.md) for an AI-driven SEO ranking predictor & recommendation system.

INTENDED SCOPE:
- Domain: Developer documentation pages (docs.python.org, react.dev, Stripe docs, MDN, etc.)
- Scale: ~1500 scraped pages — narrowed-domain pivot from the original 50K-row proposal, executing CIS 2450 TA Ricky's 3/29/2026 email guidance to focus on a specific topic area when full-scale collection is rate-limited. Pivot will be cited in README.md and data/README.md.
- Prediction target: Binary classification — does this documentation page appear in the top-10 Google SERP results for its primary topic query (query derived from the page's <title> tag)? Single, specific, measurable target — explicitly addresses Shivani's feedback that the original proposal was too vague.

PLANNED DATA SOURCES (2 distinct):
1. Scraped doc-site HTML (async scraper, rate-limited, robots.txt-respecting)
2. SerpApi free tier OR Brave Search API for SERP rankings ground truth

PLANNED FEATURES (well above 7-10 minimum):
- Content: TF-IDF, keyword density, readability (Flesch), text length
- Metadata: title length, meta description length/presence
- Structural: header counts (H1/H2/H3), internal/external link counts, image count, alt-text coverage
- Graph: PageRank, HITS hub/authority, in-degree, out-degree, clustering coefficient (NetworkX directed graph from internal links)

PLANNED MODEL SWEEP (8 models, all with documented justification):
1. Logistic regression (baseline)
2. Random Forest
3. Gradient Boosting (sklearn)
4. XGBoost
5. LightGBM
6. CatBoost
7. SVM with RBF
8. Shallow MLP in PyTorch (hits the Colab/checkpoint requirement)

All using StratifiedKFold CV + RandomizedSearchCV. Unified evaluator returns precision/recall/F1/ROC-AUC/PR-AUC/confusion matrix. Final comparison table + clear winner + SHAP analysis on winner. MODELING_DECISIONS.md will log every kept/dropped decision with prior-result reasoning.

PLANNED EDA (5 rubric-targeted charts in notebooks/01_eda.ipynb, exported as ≥300 DPI PNG to assets/charts/):
- Class balance (justifies F1/ROC-AUC over accuracy)
- Distribution plots per numeric feature with top-10 vs not-top-10 hue
- Feature-vs-target correlation heatmap
- PageRank distribution histogram (log-scale x)
- TF-IDF top terms by class
Each chart gets a markdown caption explicitly tying it to a modeling decision.

PLANNED DASHBOARD (Streamlit, 20 pts):
- URL input → live feature extraction (scrape on demand)
- Top-10 probability gauge/progress bar
- SHAP top-5 contributing features panel
- Recommendation panel (actionable text suggestions)
- What-if simulator with sliders + live re-prediction (rubric differentiator)
- Custom CSS for typography
- Sidebar nav: Predict, Recommendations, What-if, About
- Demo data fallback so live demo never breaks

PLANNED PRESENTATION (10 pts, 8-10 min hard window, both members speak):
- PDF slides, 8-10 min recording, no code on slides, no sped-up audio
- Sections: objective+value (30s), data+pivot (1m), 3-5 EDA charts (2m), modeling progression (3m), implications (1m), limitations (1m), live demo (1m)
- ≥300 DPI charts, seaborn whitegrid or custom matplotlib stylesheet

PLANNED CODEBASE STRUCTURE (modular, docstrings + type hints throughout):
/
├── README.md (CIS 2450 main, maps each rubric req to file path)
├── readme.txt (NETS 1500 one-pager)
├── USER_MANUAL.md (NETS 1500 user manual w/ screenshots)
├── MODELING_DECISIONS.md (living log)
├── requirements.txt, .env.example
├── data/{raw,interim,processed}/, data/README.md
├── src/{scraping,features,graph,models,recommendations,dashboard}/
├── notebooks/{01_eda,02_graph_analysis,03_model_comparison}.ipynb
├── tests/test_{features,graph,models}.py
└── presentation/{slides.pdf, recording.mp4}

PLANNED PHASE ORDERING (for ROADMAP.md):
Phase 1: Data layer (scraping + SERP) + 2-source documentation
Phase 2: Feature engineering (content/metadata/structural)
Phase 3: Graph layer (PageRank/HITS) + graph features
Phase 4: EDA + 3-5 rubric charts
Phase 5: Modeling sweep (all 8 models with justification)
Phase 6: Recommendation system + SHAP
Phase 7: Streamlit dashboard with what-if simulator
Phase 8: NETS 1500 deliverables (USER_MANUAL.md + readme.txt) — ship to Gradescope tonight 4/29
Phase 9: CIS 2450 polish (README, MODELING_DECISIONS.md cleanup, codebase review)
Phase 10: Slide deck + recording (Ayush leads, Rahil reviews) — by 4/30 11:59pm

DEADLINE CONTEXT:
- Today is 2026-04-29
- NETS 1500 (85 pts) due TONIGHT 4/29 — minimum viable
- CIS 2450 (143 pts) due TOMORROW 4/30 11:59pm — 90% of effort goes here

WORK BREAKDOWN:

- Ayush: EDA + visualizations, kernel+neural models, evaluation framework, recommendations, dashboard frontend+polish, slide deck

QUESTION FOR THE COMPLIANCE TOOL:

## Grading framework

The HW5 project is worth 85 points. NETS 1500 cares about **course-topic
integration** — the project must use at least one (typically two) of:
- Graph and graph algorithms
- Social Networks
- Physical Networks (Internet)
- Information Networks (World Wide Web) ← natural fit for SEO project
- Document Search / Information Retrieval ← also a natural fit
- Game Theory, Auctions, Matching Markets
- Advanced topics (privacy, recommendations, etc.)

### Project type — this is an Implementation Project

Required deliverables:
1. **Code** — well-organized, runnable.
2. **User manual with screenshots** — describes how to run + use the program,
   covering all functionality and options. This is NOT optional.

### Course-topic integration check

The SEO project should naturally hit:
- **Information Networks (WWW)** — by definition, since you're working with
  webpages, links, and rankings.
- **Document Search / Information Retrieval** — TF-IDF, ranking, content
  scoring all qualify.
- **Graph algorithms** — per Shivani's feedback, this needs to be made
  concrete. Options: PageRank or HITS over a link graph from your scraped
  pages, shortest paths between linked documents, community detection on
  topic clusters. Pick at least one and integrate it explicitly.
- **Recommendations** — the recommendation system fits this directly.

### readme.txt requirement (1 page, mandatory)

Must contain:
- Project name + 4–5 sentence description.
- Which categories from the list above were used.
- Work breakdown — who did what.
- Concrete details on AI usage (if any).

### Address Shivani's specific feedback

Per her email:
1. Did you make the prediction target specific (exact metric, not vague
   "how well it ranks")?
2. Did you clearly explain where webpage data comes from? Specific datasets,
   APIs, or crawled domains?
3. Did you specify which graph algorithms you actually used? This was
   explicitly flagged.
4. If proposal changed since submission, is the change documented and was
   Shivani kept in the loop?

### Submission

Gradescope, due April 29 11:59pm (note: NETS deadline is April 29, CIS 2450
is April 30 — don't conflate them).

For each component: tentative score, gaps, concrete actions.

## Final unified instruction

Produce a single prioritized punch-list across both courses. Flag anything that would cost points in either rubric, anything that contradicts TA feedback, and anything that's a hard-fail risk (e.g., < 50K rows for CIS 2450, missing graph algorithm for NETS 1500, presentation outside 8–10 min). Sort by severity and by deadline (NETS 1500 = 4/29, CIS 2450 = 4/30).
